events.event: The Glorious First of June: The first and largest fleet action of the naval conflict between Great Britain and the French First Republic.
The first television licences are issued in Great Britain, costing £2.
The birth of Mike Campbell, the English musician and member of the Dirty Knobs.
The coronation of Queen Elizabeth II takes place in Westminster Abbey.
Thomas Hardy, the novelist and poet, is born in Dorset.
European football governing body UEFA lifts the ban on English clubs playing in Europe following the Heysel Stadium disaster.
The Duke of Windsor marries Wallis Simpson in France following his abdication from the British throne.
The birth of George V, King of the United Kingdom and the British Dominions.
Interpol is re-established in Brussels with significant British cooperation.
The Dunkirk evacuation ends, with over 338,000 Allied troops rescued from France.
The birth of King George III at Norfolk House in London.
Suffragette Emily Davison is fatally injured after being hit by the King's horse at the Epsom Derby.
The United Kingdom holds its first national referendum on whether to remain in the European Economic Community (EEC).
The birth of Adam Smith, the Scottish economist and philosopher, in Kirkcaldy.
Lord Kitchener, the Secretary of State for War, dies when HMS Hampshire sinks off the Orkney Islands.
D-Day: British and Allied forces land on the beaches of Normandy during Operation Overlord.
The Young Men's Christian Association (YMCA) is founded by George Williams in London.
The first Transit of Venus is observed by British astronomers including Nevil Maskelyne.
The Great Reform Act receives Royal Assent, significantly changing the electoral system in England and Wales.
The death of Alan Turing, the pioneering British computer scientist and codebreaker.
The Field of the Cloth of Gold meeting begins between Henry VIII of England and Francis I of France.
The first recorded Viking raid on England takes place at the monastery on Lindisfarne.
George Orwell's dystopian novel 'Nineteen Eighty-Four' is published in London.
The birth of Tim Berners-Lee, the British computer scientist who invented the World Wide Web.
The death of Charles Dickens, the celebrated Victorian author, at Gad's Hill Place.
The first sitting of the House of Lords is televised.
The birth of Gerald Dixey, the English stage and television actor.
The birth of Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh, in Corfu.
The birth of David Pinner, the British actor and novelist.
The birth of James Francis Edward Stuart (The Old Pretender), sparking the Warming Pan Scandal.
The marriage of Henry VIII and Catherine of Aragon takes place at Greenwich.
The birth of Sir Jackie Stewart, the legendary Scottish racing driver.
The death of George I, the first Hanoverian King of Great Britain.
Margaret Thatcher wins her third consecutive general election victory.
The birth of Robert Browning, the major Victorian poet.
George H.W. Bush is born; his later political ties with the UK would define the 'Special Relationship'.
Peasants' Revolt: Rebels led by Wat Tyler enter London and burn the Savoy Palace.
The birth of James Clerk Maxwell, the Scottish physicist who formulated electromagnetic theory.
The birth of Dorothy L. Sayers, the English crime writer and poet.
The Falklands War ends as Argentine forces surrender at Port Stanley.
The Battle of Naseby: The New Model Army defeats the Royalists during the English Civil War.
John Logie Baird, the Scottish inventor of television, dies in Bexhill-on-Sea.
King John seals the Magna Carta at Runnymede.
Charles Goodyear receives a patent for vulcanised rubber, a technology crucial for British industry.
The death of Edward the Black Prince, heir to the English throne.
The Battle of Quatre Bras takes place two days before Waterloo.
The Metropolitan Police Act is passed, establishing the first modern police force in London.
The birth of Stan Laurel, the English comic actor and half of Laurel and Hardy.
The last public execution by guillotine in France is observed by future British secret agent Christopher Lee.
The birth of John Wesley, the English cleric and founder of Methodism.
The birth of Iwan Thomas, the British sprinter and Olympic medallist.
The Battle of Waterloo: The Duke of Wellington defeats Napoleon Bonaparte.
Winston Churchill delivers his 'This was their finest hour' speech to the House of Commons.
The birth of Paul McCartney, the legendary English singer-songwriter and Beatle.
The birth of James VI and I, the first monarch to rule both Scotland and England.
The birth of Jeremy Paxman, the influential British broadcaster and journalist.
The first ever episode of 'The Archers' is recorded for pilot broadcast.
Queen Victoria ascends to the throne following the death of her uncle, William IV.
The Black Hole of Calcutta incident occurs in India.
The birth of Lionel Richie; his work has had significant influence on UK pop charts.
The birth of Prince William, Duke of Cambridge, at St Mary's Hospital, London.
The Manchester Baby, the world's first stored-program computer, runs its first program.
The birth of Pete Rock; while American, his production heavily influenced the UK hip hop scene.
The HMT Empire Windrush arrives at Tilbury Docks, marking the start of the Windrush generation.
The Royal Observatory, Greenwich is established by King Charles II.
The first cricket match at the current Lord's Cricket Ground is played.
The United Kingdom votes to leave the European Union in the Brexit referendum.
The birth of Alan Turing, the father of theoretical computer science, in London.
The Battle of Bannockburn begins between the forces of Robert the Bruce and Edward II.
The Battle of Bannockburn ends with a decisive victory for Scotland over the English.
The coronation of Henry VIII and Catherine of Aragon takes place at Westminster Abbey.
The birth of Mick Fleetwood, the English musician and co-founder of Fleetwood Mac.
The Korean War begins, with significant British involvement as part of UN forces.
George Orwell, author of 'Animal Farm' and '1984', is born in India.
The birth of George Michael, the influential English singer-songwriter.
'Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone' is first published in the UK.
William IV ascends to the throne following the death of George IV.
The birth of Maurice Wilkes, the British computer scientist who developed EDSAC.
Tony Blair resigns as Prime Minister and is succeeded by Gordon Brown.
The Battle of Dettingen: The last time a British monarch (George II) personally led troops into battle.
The birth of Nigella Lawson, the English food writer and broadcaster.
The coronation of Queen Victoria takes place at Westminster Abbey.
The birth of Henry VIII at Greenwich Palace.
The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand, triggering World War I with subsequent UK entry.
The original Globe Theatre in London burns to the ground during a performance of Henry VIII.
The first census is taken in the UK.
The birth of Robert Hardy, the acclaimed English actor.
The Night of the Long Knives in Germany is reported extensively in the British press, shifting public opinion.
The birth of Mike Tyson; his fights in the UK were major sporting events.
The death of Herbert Biberman, an influential figure whose work reached the UK film industry.
